coredata.dc:title: Samarium doped carbon dots for near-infrared photo-therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Huixian
Yin, Yanfei
Xu, Hao
Qu, Xuan
Wang, Hongliang
An, Zhongfu
coredata.dc:description: Photo-assisted tumor therapy, encompassing photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT), has garnered considerable attention as a promising approach for tumor treatment owing to its stimuli-responsive nature and noninvasive characteristics. Carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as a novel type of nanomaterial with great potential in the field of anticancer therapy. However, the development of multifunctional CDs nano-platforms that integrate multiple therapeutic performance remains a challenge, especially in near-infrared (NIR) window. In this study, we synthesized samarium-doped carbon dots (Sm/CDs) via a one-step hydrothermal process for fluorescence imaging guided PTT/PDT in NIR-II window. Sm/CDs demonstrated remarkable absorption and emission properties in the NIR region, enabling efficient generation of heat energy, superoxide (•O2 –) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) under NIR irradiation. The photothermal conversion efficiencies of Sm/CDs reached up to 34.12 % and 24.38 % when exposed to 808 nm and 1060 nm wavelengths, respectively. Moreover, long-term toxicity experiments conducted in vivo as well as fluorescence experiments confirmed the potential of such functional CDs for fluorescence imaging and excellent biocompatibility. It is noteworthy that the tumor is nearly eradicated when exposed to either 808 nm or 1060 nm, demonstrating significant efficacy in both scenarios. This study provides a multifunctional CDs nano-platform that offers a promising bimodal phototherapeutic strategy to enhance the synergistic treatment of tumors through PDT and PTT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of carbon dot materials. (a) TEM images of Sm/CDs, inset: HRTEM images of Sm/CDs (b) Particle size distribution of Sm/CDs. (c) XRD images of CDs and Sm/CDs. (d-f) XPS high-resolution C1s, F1s and Sm3d (above: Sm/CDs, below: CDs). (g) UV–vis-NIR spectra of CDs, Sm/CDs and Sm3+. (h) Excitation and emission spectra of Sm/CDs. (i) Emission spectra of Sm/CDs by changing excitation wavelength from 490 nm to 570 nm.

Performance of PDT and PTT. (a) Photothermal curves of Sm/CDs with different power at a fixed concentration (1.0 mg·mL−1). (b) Linear relationship between − ln θ and time under 808 nm and 1060 laser irradiation. (c) MB degradation due to ·OH generation under different conditions. (d) Change of DHR123 fluorescent intensity owing to ·O2 – generation under different conditions. ESR spectra of samples under 808 nm and 1060 nm laser irradiation detecting the signal of ·OH (e) and·O2 – (f). (g) Warming and cooling cycles of Sm/CDs (1.0 mg·mL−1) under 1060 nm (2.0 W·cm−2) irradiation.

(a) CLSM images of Calcein-AM (green, live cells) and propidium iodide (red, dead cells) double-stained DU145 cells after different treatments. scale bar = 200 μm. (b) Viability of DU145 cells incubated at various concentrations of Sm/CDs (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 and 1.0 mg · mL−1) with and without 1064 or 808 nm irradiation (2.0 W · cm−2) for 10 min. (c) Confocal FL images of cellular uptake of CDs, Sm/CDs by DU145 cells, scale bar = 200 μm. (d) CLSM images of ROS generation under different treatments, scale bar = 50 μm. (e) In vivo fluorescence imaging of tumor-bearing nude mice at the corresponding time after Sm/CDs injection, red circles indicate the tumor area. (f) Fluorescence images and intensities of major organs and tumors in DU145 hormonal mice 24 h after Sm/CDs injection. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001).

(a) Infrared thermal images and (b) Corresponding temperature elevation at the tumor sites of DU145 tumor-bearing mice in the PBS group and Sm/CDs + laser group under 808 or 1064 nm irradiation with 2.0 W·cm−2 at different time intervals. (c) Time-dependent tumor volume of tumor-bearing nude mice under different treatments. (d) Photographs of tumor-bearing nude mice at day 14 and H&E staining of tumor sections from each group. scale bar = 200 μm. (e) Photos of tumors dissected from each group on the 14th day after photothermal treatment. (f) Time-dependent body weight of tumor-bearing nude mice under different treatments.
Schematic illustration of preparation of Sm/CDs and FL-guided photothermal therapy of tumor.

head.ce:title: Samarium doped carbon dots for near-infrared photo-therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo-assisted tumor therapy, encompassing photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT), has garnered considerable attention as a promising approach for tumor treatment owing to its stimuli-responsive nature and noninvasive characteristics. Carbon dots (CDs) have recently emerged as a novel type of nanomaterial with great potential in the field of anticancer therapy. However, the development of multifunctional CDs nano-platforms that integrate multiple therapeutic performance remains a challenge, especially in near-infrared (NIR) window. In this study, we synthesized samarium-doped carbon dots (Sm/CDs) via a one-step hydrothermal process for fluorescence imaging guided PTT/PDT in NIR-II window. Sm/CDs demonstrated remarkable absorption and emission properties in the NIR region, enabling efficient generation of heat energy, superoxide (•O2 –) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) under NIR irradiation. The photothermal conversion efficiencies of Sm/CDs reached up to 34.12 % and 24.38 % when exposed to 808 nm and 1060 nm wavelengths, respectively. Moreover, long-term toxicity experiments conducted in vivo as well as fluorescence experiments confirmed the potential of such functional CDs for fluorescence imaging and excellent biocompatibility. It is noteworthy that the tumor is nearly eradicated when exposed to either 808 nm or 1060 nm, demonstrating significant efficacy in both scenarios. This study provides a multifunctional CDs nano-platform that offers a promising bimodal phototherapeutic strategy to enhance the synergistic treatment of tumors through PDT and PTT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The incidence and mortality rates of malignant tumors have shown upward trend, posing a significant threat to human health and life [1,2]. Currently, traditional tumor treatment methods like radiotherapy, chemotherapy and surgery result in irreversible damages to the human body [3,4]. Therefore, there is an urgent need for the development of advanced therapies. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) are gaining more attention due to their advantages of noninvasive, local treatment modalities and minimal toxicity [5–7]. PDT involves a process wherein photosensitizers undergo electron transfer upon suitable illumination, leading to generation of cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) and subsequently inducing apoptosis in tumor cells [8,9]. PTT employs photothermal agents that are exposed to an external light source, enabling the conversion of light into heat. This localized heating effectively eradicates biologically active molecules within the cells and consequently eliminates cancerous cells [10–12]. Hence, the efficacy of PTT depends on the photothermal conversion performance of these agents.

Notably, a single mode of PTT or PDT has certain limitations to some extent. PTT is susceptible to temperature-dependent issues, where high temperature results in damage to normal tissue and inflammation in tumor tissue. Conversely, insufficient temperature allows stress-induced heat shock protein to easily repair damaged cancer cells, leading to tumor recurrence [13]. On the other hand, type-II PDT relies on limited availability of O2 due to rapid consumption and vascular abnormalities in tumor tissues [14]. To address the limitation of a single treatment modality, synergistic treatment models combining PDT and PTT are now being widely explored [15–17].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescent photothermal material, have attracted considerable attention due to their low biotoxicity, easy modifiability and remarkable optical properties [18,19]. The exceptional fluorescent characteristics of CDs can be utilized for fluorescence imaging to guide tumor therapy. Jia and co-authors employed a solvothermal method to synthesize CDs nanoparticles for bi-functional FL/PA imaging of tumors and synergistic PDT/PTT. Furthermore, efficient generation of 1O2 and heat can be achieved under 635 nm irradiation, enabling effective synergistic PTT/PDT treatment for tumors [20]. Sun's group has successfully developed a nano-platform by assembling CDs-Ce6 and Cu2+, which exhibit fluorescence under 660 nm light irradiation. This innovative approach capitalizes on the tumor microenvironment to synergistically enhance PTT and PDT for effective tumor treatment. Importantly, this strategy not only harnesses the combined benefits of PDT and PTT but also circumvents the limitations associated single-modality therapy [21–23]. Qu and his co-authors prepared CDs nanoparticles using the solvothermal method, employing urea, citric acid and dimethyl sulfoxide. Upon intravenous injection in mice followed by irradiation with a 655 nm laser, significant therapeutic effects were observed, resulting in substantial tumor ablation [10]. Currently, the majority of CDs exhibit absorption and emission wavelengths predominantly centered around 600 nm. The near-infrared (NIR) bio-window has gained significant attention due to its reduced harm towards normal tissues, substantial penetration depth and enhanced resolution for bio-imaging. Consequently, it is crucial to enhance the absorption and emission capabilities of CDs towards the NIR-I (650–950 nm) or even the NIR-II (1000–1700 nm) bio-window.

NIR light possesses insufficient energy to directly stimulate photosensitizers for ROS generation, particularly in the case of NIR-II light. The integration of upconversion functionality has been employed to achieve NIR excitation. It is widely recognized that the progressive filling of 4f orbitals in rare earth elements results in an anti-Stokes shift with NIR light excitation. With their extended luminescence lifetimes and narrow emission bands, these elements possess the capability to minimize tissue autofluorescence, making them a promising candidate for luminescent material [24,25]. Tian's group successfully synthesized terbium-doped CDs (Tb-CDs) using a one-step hydrothermal method [26]. The results revealed significant enhancements in the quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of Tb-CDs, accompanied by red-shifted fluorescence emission peaks. By utilizing Tb-CDs as a probe for (dipicolinic acid) DPA assay, an impressive detection limit of 44 nM was achieved. The aforementioned research suggests that the up-conversion of rare earth elements enables the expansion of the absorption and emission of the material to NIR-I or even NIR-II bio-window [27]. However, most studies primarily focus on improvement of optical properties of CDs by doping with rare earth ions, such as improving the fluorescence lifetime, increasing the fluorescence quantum yield, and broadening the fluorescence spectrum. There is limited report on investigation of the effect of such doping on ROS generation and photothermal properties.

Herein, we synthesized a novel Sm3+ doped CDs (Sm/CDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ), exhibiting remarkable absorption and emission properties. As expected, efficient generation of ROS and photothermal conversion capabilities for fluorescence imaging-guided PTT/PDT were obtained under excitation by NIR light, making them promising photosensitizers for PDT. The photothermal conversion efficiencies of Sm/CDs reached up to 34.12 % and 24.38 % when exposed to 808 nm and 1060 nm wavelengths, respectively. Impressively, the synergistic effect of PDT and PTT enhanced the rapid ablation of tumor and inhibited its recurrence effectively when exposed to either 808 nm or 1060 nm. It provides a broad prospect for the development of multifunctional phototherapeutic agents that can effectively perform the combined therapy of PTT and PDT for tumors.

Sm/CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method [28]. Initially, 2 g of citric acid and 4 g of urea were dissolved in 40 mL DMF in a beaker. Subsequently, NH4F (0.1950 g) and SmCl3·6H2O (0.9287 g) were added into the above mixture, which was then vigorously sonicated until homogeneity. Then, the resulting mixture was transferred to a 50 mL reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 4 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the red suspension was transferred to excess NaOH solution (50 mg·mL−1), following sonication for 5 min, the black precipitate was obtained by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 10 min. It was subsequently washed three time with dilute hydrochloric acid and deionized water, respectively, and finally freeze-dried to obtain a black powdery sample.

The photothermal performance of Sm/CDs with different Sm3+ doping percentages (2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 6.0 mol%), different powers (0.2, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.2 1.5, 2.0 W·cm−2), different concentrations (0.05, 0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 1.00 mg·mL−1) was recorded by a thermal imager.

The ability of Sm/CDs to generate •OH was detected using methylene blue (MB) as a probe. A mixture of Sm/CDs (1 mg·mL−1, 100 μL) and MB aqueous solution was subjection to irradiation with 808 and 1060 nm lasers for 10 min, while the absorbance changes were recorded at two-minute intervals. Additionally, the absorbance changes of pure MB under identical laser irradiation conditions were examined in order to eliminate any potential influence from the laser on MB degradation.

Dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR 123) reacts with •O2 – and emits strong fluorescence at an emission wavelength of 525 nm. So, DHR 123 used as a specific probe for the detection of •O2 – [29]. To investigate its fluorescence properties, DHR123 (1 Mm, 1 μL) was dispersed in a Sm/CDs suspension (0.3 mg·mL−1, 3 mL), subsequently, the mixed solution was exposed to lasers emitting at wavelengths of both 808 nm and 1060 nm. The resulting fluorescence intensity was measured at intervals. The mixed solution was supplemented with Vc (5 Mm, 10 μL) prior to laser irradiation for the •O2 – quenching experiment. The fluorescence intensity of the DHR123 solution with Sm/CDs was recorded separately under excitation by both the 808 nm and 1060 nm laser.

The generation of ROS was further verified by ESR. 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolineN-oxide (DMPO, 100 mM) was employed as the radical trapping agent. Sm/CDs were placed in methanol and aqueous solutions of DMPO (100 mM, 1 mL), respectively. The ESR profiles of the samples were detected after 10 min of laser irradiation at 808 and 1060 nm.

The antibacterial activity of sterile samples of Sm/CDs was assessed using the plate coating method with Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria. Pure bacteria were initially incubated in liquid medium overnight in a rotary shaker at 37 °C to achieve a bacterial concentration of 1 × 108. The Sm/CDs samples were sterilized by exposing them to UV light for 30 min. these samples were then mixed with a diluted bacterial solution (1 × 105) for laser irradiation. Subsequently, the illuminated bacterial solution was further diluted to 1 × 103 and 80 μL of this solution was aspirated to coat the plates. The coated bacterial Petri dishes were subsequently incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a thermostat-controlled environment.
Cell culture: Human prostate cancer cells (DU145) were cultured in gibco medium (DMEM) containing 10 % FBS, 1 % penicillin, and streptomycin at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 and 95 % air.

In vitro cytotoxicity tests: The cytotoxicity of Sm/CDs was determined using 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assary. DU145 cells (1 × 104 cells/well) were inoculated in 96-well plates and incubated in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. PBS and various concentrations (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0 mg·mL−1) of Sm/CDs were added to the cells and co-incubated for 4 h, then the cells were washed with PBS to remove unbound samples. The experimental groups were then exposed to leases at wavelengths od either 808 nm or 1060 nm for 10 min each at an intensity of 2.0 W·cm−2. After irradiation, the cells were further incubated for another 24 h before being stained with MTT stain. The supernatant from each well was aspirated after 4 h, and cell survival was determined by measuring absorbance at a wavelength of 492 nm following additional of DMSO.

Moreover, the biocompatibility of Sm/CDs was assessed by a hemolysis assay. The Sm/CDs sample (1.0 mg·mL−1) were dissolved in PBS for 24 h. Subsequently, 1 mL of prepared Sm/CDs solution was placed into EP tubes, PBS and DI water, respectively. Orbital blood of mice (ICR) was then added to the EP tubes and allowed to incubate for 1 h to observe any potential hemolytic effects.

Staining of live and dead cells: Live-dead cell staining was conducted to observe the anti-tumor efficacy of Sm/CDs at the cellular level. DU145 cells were seeded in 24-well plates (5 × 104 cells/well) and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, the old medium was replaced with fresh medium containing CDs and Sm/CDs (0.5 mg·mL−1), respectively, followed by co-incubated for 4 h to facilitate cellular uptake. the cells were then rinsed with PBS and supplemented with fresh medium. For the experimental group, laser irradiation at wavelengths of 808 nm and 1060 nm (2.0 W·cm−2) was applied for a duration of 10 min, while the control groups were kept under dark conditions for an equivalent period of time. Afterwards, Calcein-AM/PI staining solution was added for a duration of 30 min before imaging using laser confocal microscopy.

Cell uptake of Sm/CDs: DU145 cells (5 × 104 cell/well) were seeded in a 24-well plate and cultured for 24 h. Subsequently, the culture medium was replaced with fresh medium containing CDs and Sm/CDs (0.5 mg·mL−1). The cells were then incubated for an additional 4 h to facilitate cellular uptake. Afterward, the cells were gently washed with PBS and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde aqueous solution. Following fixation, the cells underwent further washing steps before being stained with DAPI. Finally, confocal laser scanning microscopy was employed to observe the fluorescence of the samples.

Intracellular ROS production: DU145 cells (5 × 104 cell/well)) were seeded in 24-well plates and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, the old medium was replaced with fresh medium containing Sm/CDs (0.5 mg·mL−1). Following overnight incubation, the medium containing Sm/CDs was aspirated and the cells were washed with PBS prior to adding fresh medium. The cells were then subjected to treatment either under dark conditions or near-infrared lasers (808 nm or 1060 nm, 2.0 W/cm2) for a duration of 10 min. Subsequently, DCFH-DA staining was performed by addition of the dye followed by laser confocal detection after a period of 30 min to detected ROS levels.

Animal Tumor Model: The animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals of “Taiyuan University of Technology” and they were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of the International Animal Welfare Standards (tyut202205001). Male nude mice weight approximately 20 g were obtained from Shanxi Medical University. The right side was subcutaneously inoculated with a DU145 cell suspension (2 × 106 cells dispersed in 100 µL). once the tumor volume reached about 100 mm3, animal studies were performed.

In vivo biosafety: To evaluate the long-term biosafety of Sm/CDs in vivo, toxicity analyses were conducted on healthy mice over a 14-day period. Intravenous administration of PBS and Sm/CDs (5 mg·kg−1 or 10 mg·kg−1) were performed on the mice. Subsequently, serum and organs were collected for analysis of serum biochemistry including alkaline phosphatase (ALP), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), creatinine (CR) and urea at 1, 7, and, 14 days post-injection. Additionally, major organs from the mice were subjected to hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining followed by observation under light microscopy.

In vivo fluorescence imaging: The feasibility of in vivo fluorescence imaging of Sm/CDs in nude mice was investigated using the IVIS spectral imaging system (IVIS Lumina III; Perki Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA). Mude mice were intratumorally injected with PBS and Sm/CDs at a dose of 0.5 mg·kg−1 each. In vivo fluorescence imaging was conducted at 1, 2, 4, 8, and 24 h post-injection using the IVIS Lumina III Mouse Imaging System. After a period of 24 h, euthanasia was performed on the mice and the fluorescence intensity of major organs and tumors was detected.

In vivo anti-tumor assay: Tumor-bearing nude mice were allocated into six groups (three mice per group) for in vivo anti-tumor experiments. The group consisted of PBS, 808 nm, 1060 nm, Sm/CDs, Sm/CDs + 808 nm and Sm/CDs + 1060 nm treatment. Subsequently, intratumor injection of either PBS or Sm/CDs (10 mg·kg−1) was performed by irradiation of the tumor site using an 808 or 1060 nm (2.0 W·cm−2) laser for a duration of 10 min. The temperature at the tumor site was monitored using a photothermal imager. Mice body weight was recorded every two days while the s tumor volume in each group was measured and calculated as V = width2 × length /2. After a total treatment period of 14 days, euthanasia was conducted to remove tumors and major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys), which were subsequently stained with H&E.

Statistical Analysis: All experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the experimental data were assessed as mean ± standard deviation. In all the statistical evaluation, significant levels of *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 denoted significant, highly significant and extremely significant, respectively.

Firstly, the morphology of Sm/CDs was characterized by the transmission electron microscope (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1 a, the Sm/CDs were uniformly dispersed with a spherical shape and zero-dimensional characteristics, possessing an average size of approximately 2.45 nm (Fig. 1b). In addition, a high-resolution TEM image revealed lattice fringes with a resolution of 0.33 nm that corresponded well to the graphitic carbon (002) diffraction surface of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1a inset) [30]. Based on Fig. S1a and b, the particle size of CDs was found to be around 2.50 nm, while the lattice stripe is 0.31 nm (Fig. S1 inset). It can be inferred that the doping with Sm3+ had minimal impact on the structure of the CDs. The XRD patterns of samples (Fig. 1c) demonstrate that there is coincidence in diffraction peaks between CDs and Sm/CDs, indicating no structure changes due to the introduction of Sm3+. Furthermore, it is worth noting that peak intensities are significantly enhanced in Sm/CDs compared to CDs, suggesting an enhancement in crystallinity through doping with Sm3+ [31]. Additionally, a distinct peak at 27.4° confirms that Sm/CDs exhibit a characteristic (002) diffraction pattern associated with graphitic carbon [32], which aligns consistently with HR-TEM image.

The composition of elements and valence states of the material were further determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As depicted in Fig. S2a, the survey spectra indicated that Sm/CDs consisted of C, N, O, F and Sm, in comparison, CDs mainly composed of C, N, O and F. Fig. 1d displayed four peaks in C1s spectrum at 289.48, 287.57, 285.15 and 284.8 eV corresponding to the presence of C-F, O = C-N, C-O/C-N, and C-C bonds, respectively [33,34]. Upon doping with Sm3+, a blue shift was observed in the binding energy of C1s, indicating an increase in electron density around Sm3+ and a decrease in electron cloud density for CDs [35]. In the F1s spectrum (Fig. 1e), a prominent peak observed at 688.1 eV indicates the presence of a C-F bond in the structure, with higher F1s peak intensity observed for Sm/CDs compared to CDs alone. Additionally, in the high-resolution Sm3d spectrum (Fig. 1f), two characteristic peaks located at energies of 1084 and 1108 eV are assigned to the Sm 3d5/2 and Sm 3d3/2 states, respectively [36]. The O1S band can be deconvoluted into two distinct peaks at 533.11 and 531.31 eV, which correspond to C-OH/C-O-C and C = O functionalities, respectively (Fig. S2b) [13]. For the N1s spectrum (Fig. S2c), the presence of two peaks at 401.51 and 389.81 eV can be attributed to pyrrolic and pyridinic N, respectively [37].

Next, the functional groups of samples were analyzed using FT-IR spectra. As illustrated in Fig. S3, a broad peak observed at approximately 3310 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H [27]. Additionally, the absorption peak at 2954 cm−1 arises from the stretching vibration of –CH3 within the structure of samples [38]. Furthermore, characteristic stretching vibrations including C = O, C-N, C-F, and C-O can be attributed to bands observed at 1642, 1358, 1261, and 1098 cm−1, respectively [30]. It is noteworthy that similar FT-IR spectra are exhibited for both CDs and Sm/CDs samples, indicating that doping with Sm3+ did not alter the surface structures of CDs.

Subsequently, we investigated the photophysical properties of the CDs. From Fig. 1g, it was found that there are two distinct low wavelength absorptions peaks at 300 and 407 nm, which are attributed to π → π* transitions and n → π* transitions of the carbon nucleus, respectively [39]. Pyridine N exhibits a strong absorption peak at 523 nm, which was consistent with the XPS results above. The presence of Sm3+ solution exhibits significant absorption at 1100 nm, which serves as the fundamental basis for achieving up-conversion luminescence in the NIR-II region upon Sm3+ doping into CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1h, the excitation and emission wavelength of Sm/CDs were determined to be 550 and 650 nm, respectively. Notably, the emission wavelength closely aligned with the biological window of NIR-I region, which offers enhanced fluorescence penetration depth. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 1i, the Sm/CDs exhibit excitation-independent behavior, indicating the formation of a singular emission cluster on the Sm/CDs surface. This characteristic effectively eliminates interference from tissue autofluorescence and shows its potential in fluorescence imaging.

Moreover, the suspension stability of the nanoparticles is determined by their Zeta potential. The Zeta potentials of CDs and Sm/CDs were − 16.63 mV and − 15.34 mV, respectively (Fig. S4). It has been observed that the nanoparticles with a higher surface charge at Zeta potential exhibit a repulsive force, resulting in stable dispersion and retention [40]. This observation aligns with the TEM results, indicting a superior dispersion of the samples.

The performance of Sm/CDs in type-I PDT was evaluated by investigating their ability to generate •OH and •O2 – using methylene blue (MB) and dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR123) as probes, respectively. Fig. S5 demonstrates a positive correlation between the degradation of MB and irradiation time. Additionally, Fig. 2 a displays the degradation of MB within a 10 min timeframe under different condition. In the control group (exposed solely 808 or 1060 nm laser), no significant decrease in MB absorbance was observed. Conversely, CDs exhibited a moderate degradation effect on MB reduction of 29.5 % and 31.7 % under an 808 and 1060 nm laser, respectively. Comparatively, Sm/CDs displayed a pronounced degradation effect on MB reduction of 55.35 % and 40.68 % when exposed to 808 and 1060 nm laser, respectively. It is confirmed that the introduction of Sm3+ enhances the •OH production capacity of CDs.

It was observed that DHR 123 can exhibit strong fluorescence upon reacting with •O2 –, thus making a suitable probe for the specific detection of •O2 –. As shown in Fig. S6, intense fluorescence was observed when exposed to both 808 and 1060 nm laser for Sm/CDs sample. The significant production of •O2 – can be attributed to the catalytic effect of photogenerated electrons on the surface of Sm/CDs in generating •O2 – from O2. To further confirm that the increase in fluorescence intensity was indeed due to •O2 – production, Vc was introduced as a free radical scavenger for •O2 –. As depicted in Fig. 2b, no fluorescence signal was detected after adding Vc, thereby demonstrating the capability of Sm/CDs to generate •O2 –. Moreover, The ROS generation of Sm/CDs was further validated under laser irradiation through electron spin resonance (ESR) detection. As depicted in Fig. 2c and d, in comparison with CDs, the addition of Sm3+ doping can significantly enhance the capacity for generating ROS, indicating their substantial potential for application in type-I PDT.

Based on the UV–vis-NIR spectra, Sm/CDs exhibited broad absorption peaks in both the NIR-I and NIR-II regions. To further assess their photothermal performance and conversion efficiency, we conducted experiments to verify the photothermal conversion capability of varying percentages of Sm3+ doping, varying concentrations of Sm/CDs and laser power densities. As shown in Fig S7, the photothermal performance exhibited a gradual increase, followed by a decrease as the doping ratio increased. The highest temperature rise was observed at a doping ratio of 3 %. Significantly, as depicted in Fig. 2e and S8, it was discovered that the temperature of Sm/CDs aqueous solution (1.0 mg·mL−1) could attain temperatures increased by 61.8 and 41.5 °C under an 808 and 1060 nm laser at a power density of 2.0 W·cm−1, respectively. This level of temperature rise is sufficient for inducing hyperthermia tumor ablation purposes. Besides, a distinct heating effect dependent on power and concentration was revealed. based on the photothermal heating and cooling curves in Fig. 2f and S9, the calculated photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of Sm/CDs in water was determined to be 34.12 % and 24.38 % under 808 and 1060 nm laser respectively, which is a relatively high level compared to other reported CDs systems, expensively exposed in 808 and 1060 nm (Fig. S10) [21,41–50]. Furthermore, the photothermal stability emerged as a crucial characteristic for photothermal agents. As illustrate in Fig. 2g and Fig. S11, the photothermal properties of Sm/CDs exhibited negligible alterations even after ten cycle of laser irradiation at 808 or 1060 nm, demonstrating the exceptional photothermal stability of Sm/CDs under NIR lasers irradiation. These results validated that Sm/CDs possess high photothermal conversion efficiency and excellent photothermal stability when exposed to both 808 and 1060 nm irradiation, respectively. This indicates that Sm/CDs hold significant potential for broad application in the field of photothermal diagnostics.

Before proceeding with further animal experiments, the in vitro anticancer effects of the as-synthesized Sm/CDs on DU145 cells were evaluated using MTT and live-dead cell staining assays. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the control group (without lasers) exhibited strong green fluorescent signals in the image. Conversely, nearly all cells in the lasers and sample group were stained red. The number of cells in the PBS + 1060 nm and PBS + 808 nm groups was smaller than that in the non-light group. It has been reported in the literature that higher light doses during cell irradiation result in decreased cell activity, but no dead cells were observed [51,52]. The viability of DU145 cells, as demonstrated in Fig. 3b, remained above 84 % following a 24 h co-incubation exclusively with Sm/CDs, indicating minimal cytotoxicity towards the cells. Conversely, a concentration-dependent induction of cell death was observed upon irradiation with either an 808 or 1060 nm laser. Notably, when the concentration of Sm/CDs was increased to 1.0 mg·mL−1, the survival rate of DU145 cells decreased to approximately 44 % and 31 % under 1060 and 808 nm irradiation, respectively. Furthermore, Sm/CDs and laser-treated cells displayed a significantly higher rate of apoptosis. These experiments effectively demonstrate the promising cytocompatibility and the exceptional PTT effect of Sm/CDs under the NIR irradiation. Furthermore, the impact of the samples on normal coagulation was evaluated by observing hemolysis in mouse venous blood when exposed to DI water, PBS, CDs and Sm/CDs solutions, respectively. It was depicted in Fig. S12 that the addition of PBS, CDs or Sm/CDs resulted in rapid and sustained coagulation ability in mice blood, confirming the excellent biocompatibility of Sm/CDs and their potential for various biological applications.

The favorable fluorescent properties, brilliant photothermal performance and outstanding ROS-generated ability of Sm/CDs have been convincingly demonstrated. Besides, the effective cellular enrichment of Sm/CDs is a prerequisite for enhanced therapeutic efficacy in vivo. Therefore, the cellular uptake capacity of Sm/CDs was evaluated using DU145 cells with a confocal scanning laser microscope (CLMS). As illustrated in Fig. 3c, a vivid red fluorescence emission was observed on DU145 cells, demonstrating a substantial accumulation of Sm/CDs within them. The remarkably efficient cell enrichment capability exhibited by Sm/CDs establishes a solid foundation for their potential application in tumor therapy in vivo.

The generation of ROS by Sm/CDs in cells was further evaluated under 808 and 1060 nm laser irradiation, respectively. As described in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity observed in the Sm/CDs group exceeded that of the CDs group. This observation confirms that Sm/CDs possess the ability to induce an excessive production of ROS within cells, thereby enhancing the desired PDT effect. Consequently, these findings provide additional confirmation regarding the potential application of Sm/CDs as photosensitizers in PDT.

To further facilitate the translation of Sm/CDs from experimental to clinical applications, it is crucial to assess their in vivo biosafety. Mouse serum and organs were collected for serum biochemical analysis at 1, 7, and, 14 days post-injection of PBS and Sm/CDs, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S13, no significant deviations were observed in the serum biochemical analyses throughout the experimental period in mice administered a sample dose of 5 mg·kg−1. However, AST, ALT, and Urea levels slightly increased after one day of injection but subsequently returned to normal in mice injected with a dosage strength of 10 mg·kg−1, indicating gradual metabolism of Sm/CDs over time. Additionally, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs and kidneys) of mice were collected on days 0, 1, 7 and 14. As depicted in Fig. S14, histological analysis using H&E staining revealed no significant abnormalities in all examined organs. These findings confirm that Sm/CDs do not exert any notable impact on the normal organ function in vivo and exhibit remarkable biosafety. This establishes a solid foundation for their potential application in tumor diagnosis and treatment within living organisms.

The suitability of Sm/CDs in vivo fluorescence imaging was further investigated to validate their efficacy as therapeutic diagnostic agents. Nude mice bearing subcutaneous DU145 cell were employed as animal models. As depicted in Fig. 3e, S15a and b, the fluorescence intensity initially increased and then decreased over time, reaching its peak at 1-hour post-injection. This observation suggests that the uptake of Sm/CDs by tumor cells reaches its maximum level within 1 h. Subsequently, the fluorescent signals gradually attenuated until they were barely detectable after 24 h, indicating near-complete metabolism of Sm/CDs at the tumor site within this timeframe. Meanwhile, to obtain a more precise assessment of the distribution of Sm/CDs in different organs of mice, we collected isolated fluorescence images and fluorescence intensity values from major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) as well as tumors 24 h post-injection. As depicted in Fig. 3f, faint fluorescent signals were observed in the major organs, indicating efficient metabolism of Sm/CDs within the organism. Importantly, the red fluorescence emitted by Sm/CDs in vivo effectively circumvents potential interference from tissue-based fluorescence to a considerable extent. This affirms the prospective application of Sm/CDs in live imaging systems.

Motivated by the results of in vitro anti-tumor experiments, further investigations were conducted to explore the in vivo anti-tumor effects of Sm/CDs. The animal experiment was designed and depicted in Fig. S16. Initially, to assess the photothermal performance in vivo, a thermal imager was employed to record and monitor the temperature elevation at the tumor site during treatment. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a and b, a time-dependent increase in temperature at the tumor site was observed upon laser irradiation. Both the Sm/CDs and laser groups demonstrated a rapid temperature increase up to 45 °C at the tumor site. Notably, when exposed to wavelengths of 808 and 1060 nm, final temperatures reached 62.3 °C and 57.2 °C, which were sufficient to cause irreversible damage to the tumor tissue.

In order to comprehensively evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of Sm/CDs, the anti-tumor experimental effects were analyzed from various perspectives. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the tumor volume consistently increased over the course of 14 days in the control groups. Remarkably, complete ablation of tumors was achieved after only two days of treatment with Sm/CDs exposed to 808 nm laser, and no recurrence observed thereafter. Besides, the H&E staining images of tumor tissues clearly demonstrated significant damage and apoptosis in both the Sm/CDs and lasers groups (Fig. 4d). Notably, when Sm/CDs were exposed with a 1060 nm laser, there was a significant reduction in tumor volume to approximately 93 % compared to its original size (Fig. 4e and S17). These findings provide compelling evidence for the remarkable anti-tumor effect resulting from synergistic photothermal therapy and photodynamic therapy using Sm/CDs. Furthermore, the organs from each group were collected and subjected to H&E staining (Fig. S18). The section images of each organ did not reveal any noticeable abnormalities. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 4f, there was no significant variation observed in the body weight of mice across all time points during the 14-day treatment period, indicating that Sm/CDs exhibited low toxicity towards mice throughout the course of the experiment.

The Sm/CDs possess outstanding ROS generation capability along with exceptional photothermal performance, resulting in a favorable antimicrobial effect through the synergistic interplay of thermal and ROS mechanisms. The inactivation ability of Sm/CDs was further investigated under 808 and 1060 nm separately. As portrayed in Fig. S19, the bactericidal efficiency increases proportionally with the power increment. When exposed to 808 nm laser radiation, a light source power of 1.0 W·cm−2 achieves a 100 % bactericidal rate after a 10 min irradiation period. Similarly, under the influence of 1060 nm laser irradiation, a power level of 2.0 W·cm−2 results in a complete eradication of bacteria after being exposed for 10 min. And the concentration required for complete inactivated of S. aureus was found to be approximately 300–400 μg·mL−1 under both 808 and 1060 nm laser, respectively. This experimental evidence establishes a solid foundation for the potential application of Sm/CDs in wound healing during the post-treatment phase [53].

In summary, we synthesized a novel type of samarium-doped carbon dots (Sm/CDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method, which exhibited remarkable near-infrared absorption and emission properties as well as exceptional biocompatibility. The as-prepared Sm/CDs showed uniform particle distribution with ultra-small particle sizes around 2.45 nm, excellent dispersion, and outstanding photothermal stability. The incorporation of Sm3+ not only significantly enhances optical imaging performance but also effectively stimulates the production of •O2 –, •OH, and heat upon exposure to 808 nm or 1060 nm laser irradiation. Cellular experiments and histological studies demonstrate their low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, highlighting their potential for biomedical applications. Besides, the aqueous solution of Sm/CDs achieved high photothermal conversion efficiencies of 34.12 % and 24.38 % under 808 nm and 1060 nm laser irradiation respectively. It is noteworthy that the tumor is nearly eradicated when exposed to either 808 nm or 1060 nm. This straightforward synthetic strategy holds promising potential for the development of multifunctional phototherapy agents capable of efficiently executing combined tumor PTT and PDT.
